passage.text:
IELTS Reading Practice
Environment & Ecology
10 Reading Passages  ·  500–600 words each  ·  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)

Contents
Passage 1: Plastic Pollution in Our Oceans
Passage 2: Deforestation and Its Consequences
Passage 3: Renewable Energy — A Clean Future
Passage 4: Wildlife Conservation — Protecting Endangered Species
Passage 5: Air Pollution and Human Health
Passage 6: Climate Change — Causes and Effects
Passage 7: Water Scarcity — A Growing Global Crisis
Passage 8: Urban Green Spaces and Environmental Benefits
Passage 9: The Impact of Fast Fashion on the Environment
Passage 10: Sustainable Agriculture — Feeding the Future

Passage 1: Plastic Pollution in Our Oceans
Topic: Plastic & Marine Pollution  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Every year, approximately eight million tonnes of plastic waste enter the world's oceans, making plastic pollution one of the most serious environmental challenges of the modern era. Plastic originates from a wide variety of sources, including single-use shopping bags, plastic bottles, food packaging, fishing nets, and industrial waste. Unlike organic materials that break down naturally in the environment, plastic is extremely resistant to decomposition. Instead of disappearing, plastic gradually breaks into smaller and smaller fragments known as microplastics — particles less than five millimetres in size — which can persist in the marine environment for hundreds, or even thousands, of years. Because plastic is lightweight and floats, it is easily carried by wind and ocean currents far from its original source, spreading the problem across every ocean on Earth.
Paragraph B
Marine animals are among the most severely affected victims of ocean plastic. Sea turtles frequently mistake floating plastic bags for jellyfish — their natural prey — and consume them, which can block their digestive systems and lead to death from starvation. Seabirds such as albatrosses collect colourful plastic pieces from the ocean surface, mistaking them for fish or squid, and feed these fragments to their chicks. Researchers have found that in some seabird colonies, over 90% of chicks contain plastic in their stomachs. Dolphins, whales, and other large marine mammals have been found dead on beaches with kilograms of plastic bags and packaging in their digestive tracts. Even microscopic sea creatures and plankton have been shown to ingest microplastic particles, meaning plastic has entered virtually every level of the marine food chain, ultimately reaching the fish and seafood that humans eat.
Paragraph C
The scale of ocean plastic pollution is difficult to comprehend. Scientists currently estimate that there are more than 150 million tonnes of plastic in the world's oceans. Every single minute, a volume of plastic equivalent to a rubbish truck is dumped into the sea somewhere in the world. One of the most striking examples of ocean plastic accumulation is the Great Pacific Garbage Patch — a vast area of floating plastic debris located in the North Pacific Ocean, between Hawaii and California. This collection of plastic waste covers an area estimated to be twice the size of the state of Texas, or roughly 1.6 million square kilometres. According to researchers, if current trends continue, the total weight of plastic in the ocean could exceed the total weight of all fish by the year 2050.
Paragraph D
Addressing the ocean plastic crisis requires effort at every level of society. Governments in many countries have introduced legislation banning or restricting single-use plastic products, such as plastic straws, cutlery, and shopping bags. In the European Union, for example, a range of single-use plastic items was banned from 2021. Beach and river clean-up projects organised by charities and volunteers collect enormous amounts of plastic before it reaches the ocean. Innovative technologies are also being developed, including systems that use floating barriers to collect plastic from ocean surfaces, and filtration methods to remove microplastics from drinking water and wastewater. However, scientists and environmentalists consistently emphasise that the most effective long-term solution is not to clean up existing plastic, but to prevent it from being produced and discarded in the first place — through recycling, product redesign, and changes in consumer behaviour.

Passage 2: Deforestation and Its Consequences
Topic: Forests & Land Use  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Forests are among the most vital ecosystems on our planet. They cover approximately 31% of Earth's total land surface and are home to an estimated 80% of all land-dwelling animal and plant species. In addition to supporting biodiversity, forests provide essential services to humanity, including clean air, clean water, stable soil, and a stable climate. However, despite their enormous importance, forests are disappearing at an alarming speed. Deforestation — the permanent removal of trees and forest cover by humans — claims approximately 4.7 million hectares of forest every year, an area roughly equivalent to the size of Switzerland. The global rate of forest loss has remained stubbornly high for decades, despite growing international awareness of the problem.
Paragraph B
The primary drivers of deforestation vary by region but share a common root in economic pressures. Agriculture is the single biggest cause worldwide, responsible for around 80% of tropical deforestation. Cattle ranching and soy farming are particularly destructive in the Amazon Basin of South America, where vast areas of rainforest are cleared every year to create pasture and cropland. Commercial logging is another significant driver, as companies harvest valuable hardwoods such as mahogany and teak for furniture, flooring, and construction. In Southeast Asia, large areas of rainforest have been cleared to plant oil palm, which produces palm oil used in thousands of food and cosmetic products. Urban expansion, road construction, and the development of mining operations also contribute to the ongoing destruction of forest land globally.
Paragraph C
The environmental consequences of deforestation are wide-reaching and deeply serious. Forests act as massive carbon stores, absorbing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere through the process of photosynthesis. When forests are burned or cut down, the carbon that has been stored in trees and soil for decades or centuries is rapidly released back into the atmosphere, significantly accelerating climate change. The Amazon rainforest alone stores an estimated 150 to 200 billion tonnes of carbon — more than 15 times the world's total annual greenhouse gas emissions. Beyond carbon storage, forests regulate regional rainfall patterns by releasing water vapour into the atmosphere. Their removal disrupts water cycles, leading to droughts and reduced river flow. Deforestation also causes severe soil erosion: without tree roots to anchor the soil, rain washes away the thin, fertile topsoil that many farming communities depend on, often making the cleared land unproductive within just a few years.
Paragraph D
Efforts to address deforestation are growing but face considerable challenges. Many national governments have established protected forest areas, national parks, and wildlife reserves where logging and land clearance are legally prohibited. International frameworks such as REDD+ (Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation) offer financial incentives to developing countries that protect and restore their forests. Some major companies have made public commitments to achieve zero-deforestation supply chains, meaning they will not purchase commodities grown on recently deforested land. Forest certification schemes, such as those run by the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC), help consumers identify wood and paper products that come from responsibly managed forests. Community-based forest management, in which local people take responsibility for protecting forests in their area, has also shown promising results in countries including Nepal, Mexico, and Ethiopia.

Passage 3: Renewable Energy — A Clean Future
Topic: Energy & Climate  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
For the majority of human history, civilisations have depended on burning fossil fuels — coal, oil, and natural gas — to power their homes, factories, and vehicles. These fuels were formed over millions of years from the remains of ancient organisms and contain enormous amounts of stored energy. However, burning fossil fuels releases large quantities of carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases, which trap heat in the Earth's atmosphere and are the primary cause of human-induced climate change. As global temperatures continue to rise and the environmental costs of fossil fuels become increasingly clear, governments, businesses, and individuals around the world are urgently seeking cleaner and more sustainable alternatives. Renewable energy — energy derived from natural processes that are continuously replenished — has emerged as the most promising solution.
Paragraph B
There are several major types of renewable energy, each with its own technology and characteristics. Solar power captures the energy of sunlight using photovoltaic panels, which convert light directly into electricity, or through solar thermal systems that use the sun's heat to warm water or generate steam. Wind energy is produced when wind turns the large blades of a turbine, generating electricity through a connected generator; wind farms can be built on land or offshore, where wind speeds are typically higher and more consistent. Hydroelectric power, one of the oldest and largest sources of renewable electricity, generates power from the kinetic energy of flowing water passing through turbines in dams. Other renewable technologies include geothermal energy, which taps heat from beneath the Earth's surface, and tidal energy, which harnesses the movement of ocean tides.
Paragraph C
The advantages of renewable energy over fossil fuels are substantial and wide-ranging. From an environmental perspective, renewable energy sources produce little or no greenhouse gas emissions or air pollution during operation, making them crucial tools in the fight against climate change. Economically, once the infrastructure is built, the "fuel" — sunlight, wind, water — is free, meaning that running costs are very low compared to fossil fuel power plants, which must continuously purchase fuel. Renewable energy also improves national energy security by reducing dependence on imported oil and gas, which can be subject to price volatility and supply disruptions. Furthermore, the rapid growth of the renewable energy sector has created millions of new jobs worldwide in manufacturing, installation, operation, and maintenance of renewable energy systems.
Paragraph D
Despite these benefits, the transition to renewable energy faces several significant challenges. One of the most important is intermittency: solar panels only produce electricity when the sun is shining, and wind turbines only generate power when the wind is blowing. This means that renewable energy systems must be paired with energy storage solutions — most commonly large-scale battery systems — to ensure a reliable supply of electricity. Currently, battery technology remains expensive and cannot yet store enough energy to power cities through extended periods of low sunlight or calm winds. Grid infrastructure also needs substantial investment to manage the variable output of renewable sources. However, the cost of solar panels and wind turbines has fallen dramatically over the past decade — by more than 80% in some cases — and energy storage technology is advancing rapidly. Most energy analysts now predict that renewable energy will provide the majority of the world's electricity by 2050.

Passage 4: Wildlife Conservation — Protecting Endangered Species
Topic: Biodiversity & Conservation  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The natural world is currently facing what many scientists describe as the sixth mass extinction event in Earth's history — a rapid, human-driven loss of biodiversity on a scale not seen since the disappearance of the dinosaurs 66 million years ago. The Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services (IPBES) estimates that approximately one million animal and plant species are currently threatened with extinction. The main causes are interconnected and largely the result of human activity: habitat destruction through deforestation and land conversion for agriculture, pollution of waterways and soils, the overexploitation of wildlife through hunting and fishing, the spread of invasive species that outcompete native animals and plants, and, increasingly, climate change, which is altering the conditions that species depend on for survival.
Paragraph B
Wildlife conservation encompasses a range of strategies designed to protect endangered species and restore biodiversity. One of the most effective tools is the creation and management of protected areas — national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, and marine reserves — where habitats are legally shielded from development, logging, and hunting. These protected zones provide safe spaces where animal populations can recover. Captive breeding programmes play an important complementary role: zoos and specialist breeding centres breed endangered species in carefully controlled environments, with the long-term goal of releasing their offspring into protected wild habitats. Anti-poaching units, equipped with modern surveillance technology including drones and camera traps, patrol reserves to catch and deter illegal hunters who target animals such as rhinos, elephants, and tigers for their horns, ivory, and bones.
Paragraph C
Several high-profile conservation success stories demonstrate that with sufficient commitment and resources, species can be brought back from the brink of extinction. The giant panda, perhaps the world's most recognisable conservation symbol, saw its status on the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) Red List upgraded from "endangered" to "vulnerable" in 2016, following decades of intensive conservation efforts in China that included habitat protection, the creation of panda reserves, and successful captive breeding. In India, the tiger population has grown from fewer than 1,500 individuals in 2006 to over 3,600 by 2023, largely due to Project Tiger, which established dedicated reserves and significantly increased enforcement against poaching. The recovery of the American bald eagle from near extinction in the 1960s — caused by the pesticide DDT weakening its eggshells — to a thriving population today is another widely celebrated example.
Paragraph D
Despite these encouraging successes, conservationists warn that the overall picture remains deeply concerning. Protected areas cover only about 17% of the world's land surface, while the majority of threatened species live outside these zones. Funding for conservation programmes is chronically insufficient relative to the scale of the problem, and political will is often lacking. Illegal wildlife trade, estimated to be worth up to 23 billion US dollars annually, remains one of the most lucrative forms of organised crime and continues to devastate populations of rare animals. Experts argue that addressing the biodiversity crisis requires not only more protected areas and better law enforcement, but also a fundamental change in humanity's relationship with nature — including reforming agricultural systems, reducing consumption, and ensuring that local communities benefit economically from conservation, giving them a direct incentive to protect wildlife.

Passage 5: Air Pollution and Human Health
Topic: Pollution & Public Health  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Air pollution is widely recognised as one of the greatest threats to public health in the modern world. The World Health Organization (WHO) estimates that polluted air is responsible for approximately 7 million premature deaths every year — a figure that exceeds the combined annual death toll from AIDS, tuberculosis, and malaria. Alarmingly, 99% of the world's population lives in areas where air quality fails to meet the WHO's recommended safety standards. Both outdoor (ambient) air pollution and indoor air pollution contribute significantly to this global health burden, though their sources and the populations they affect most differ considerably. Air pollution disproportionately affects people in low- and middle-income countries, where industrial regulation, clean fuel access, and healthcare systems tend to be weaker.
Paragraph B
The primary sources of outdoor air pollution include vehicle exhaust emissions, which release nitrogen oxides and fine particulate matter into urban air; the burning of coal and other fossil fuels in power stations and industrial facilities; and agricultural activities, including the use of nitrogen-based fertilisers and large-scale livestock farming, which produce ammonia and methane. In rapidly industrialising cities across South and Southeast Asia — including Delhi, Jakarta, and Lahore — annual average concentrations of fine particulate matter (known as PM2.5) regularly exceed WHO guidelines by ten times or more. Indoor air pollution, by contrast, is primarily caused by burning solid fuels — including wood, charcoal, crop waste, and animal dung — for cooking and heating on open fires or traditional stoves inside poorly ventilated homes. This remains a major problem across large parts of sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and rural China.
Paragraph C
The health consequences of breathing polluted air are severe and affect virtually every organ in the body. Short-term exposure to high pollution levels causes immediate symptoms including headaches, eye and throat irritation, coughing, and aggravated asthma attacks. Long-term, chronic exposure to polluted air is associated with far more serious conditions, including lung cancer, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), ischaemic heart disease, and stroke. Research has also linked long-term air pollution exposure to reduced cognitive development in children, increased risk of dementia in older adults, and adverse pregnancy outcomes including low birth weight and premature birth. Children under five, elderly people, and those with pre-existing heart or lung conditions are considered the most vulnerable groups, as their immune and respiratory systems are less able to cope with the additional burden of pollution.
Paragraph D
Tackling air pollution requires coordinated action across multiple sectors and levels of government. At the national level, governments can set and enforce strict vehicle emission standards, restrict the most polluting industrial processes, and invest in the expansion of public transport systems to reduce the number of private vehicles on roads. Transitioning the electricity sector away from coal and towards renewables dramatically reduces the release of sulphur dioxide and particulate matter from power stations. At the household level, the widespread adoption of clean cooking technologies — such as liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) stoves, solar cookers, and electric appliances — can greatly reduce indoor air pollution in developing countries. Urban tree-planting programmes also help to filter particulate matter from city air. Ultimately, reducing air pollution not only saves millions of lives annually but also brings significant economic benefits by reducing healthcare costs and increasing worker productivity.

Passage 6: Climate Change — Causes and Effects
Topic: Climate Science  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Climate change refers to long-term shifts in global temperatures and weather patterns across the Earth. Throughout the planet's history, natural factors — including variations in the Earth's orbit, volcanic eruptions, and changes in solar activity — have periodically caused significant climate variations. However, since the mid-20th century, the scientific community has reached an overwhelming consensus that human activities have become the dominant driver of the unusually rapid climate change now being observed. The primary mechanism is the emission of greenhouse gases — particularly carbon dioxide (CO₂), methane (CH₄), and nitrous oxide (N₂O) — as a result of burning fossil fuels for energy and transport, clearing and burning forests, and intensive livestock farming. These gases accumulate in the atmosphere and trap heat that would otherwise escape into space, gradually warming the planet — a phenomenon often described as the "greenhouse effect".
Paragraph B
The consequences of rising global temperatures are already highly visible and measurable. Since the pre-industrial era, average global surface temperatures have risen by approximately 1.1 to 1.2 degrees Celsius — a seemingly small figure that nevertheless has enormous implications for weather patterns, ecosystems, and sea levels worldwide. More intense and frequent heatwaves are being recorded on every continent, while extreme rainfall events and flooding have become more common in many regions. Glaciers in mountain ranges from the Alps to the Himalayas are retreating at unprecedented rates, and the Arctic ice sheet has lost roughly 75% of its summer sea ice volume since the 1970s. As glaciers and polar ice caps melt, and as warmer ocean water expands, global sea levels are rising — already by an average of 20 centimetres over the past century — threatening millions of people living in low-lying coastal zones and small island nations.
Paragraph C
Climate change poses severe threats to the natural world. Rising temperatures are pushing many plant and animal species out of their historical ranges as they attempt to track the cooler conditions they require for survival, moving towards higher altitudes or closer to the poles. Species that cannot adapt quickly enough, or that have nowhere left to move, face extinction. Ocean ecosystems are particularly threatened: as the oceans absorb both heat and carbon dioxide, they are becoming warmer and more acidic — a process known as ocean acidification. This acidification, combined with thermal stress from warming water, is causing widespread coral bleaching events, in which corals expel the colourful algae that live within them and turn white. Coral reefs support approximately 25% of all known marine species, making their degradation one of the most ecologically significant consequences of climate change.
Paragraph D
The international community has taken steps to address the climate crisis, most notably through the Paris Agreement, adopted in 2015 and ratified by nearly 200 countries. The agreement sets a collective goal of limiting global average warming to well below 2 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels, with a more ambitious target of 1.5 degrees Celsius. To meet these targets, countries are required to submit and progressively strengthen national climate plans — known as Nationally Determined Contributions — detailing how they will reduce their greenhouse gas emissions. However, scientists at the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) warn that current national commitments are insufficient to meet even the 2-degree target, and that without dramatically more rapid action to decarbonise the global economy, temperatures could rise by 3 to 4 degrees Celsius by the end of this century, with consequences that would be catastrophic and largely irreversible.

Passage 7: Water Scarcity — A Growing Global Crisis
Topic: Water Resources  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Water covers approximately 71% of the Earth's surface, giving our planet its distinctive blue appearance from space. Yet despite this apparent abundance, the vast majority of Earth's water — around 97.5% — is salt water found in the oceans and seas, which is unsuitable for drinking, irrigation, or most industrial uses without expensive treatment. Of the remaining 2.5% that is fresh water, nearly 70% is locked away in glaciers, ice caps, and permanent snow, and a further significant portion is stored as groundwater deep underground. This means that less than 1% of all water on Earth is readily accessible in rivers, lakes, and shallow aquifers for human use. Yet the demand for this precious resource continues to grow rapidly, driven by population growth, expanding agriculture, increasing industrial output, and rising standards of living that lead to greater per-capita water consumption.
Paragraph B
Water scarcity is no longer a problem confined to desert regions. The United Nations reports that around 2 billion people — roughly one quarter of the global population — currently live in countries experiencing high water stress, meaning that water demand regularly exceeds available supply. A further 3.6 billion people live in areas that face water scarcity for at least one month per year. The problem is most acute in parts of sub-Saharan Africa, the Middle East, South Asia, and northern China. However, even in wealthier regions, water scarcity is emerging as a serious challenge: severe multi-year droughts have affected California, Australia, and southern Europe in recent years, exposing the vulnerability of even developed countries to water shortages. Climate change is expected to dramatically worsen the situation, intensifying droughts in already dry regions and disrupting seasonal rainfall and snowmelt patterns that billions of people depend on.
Paragraph C
Understanding how water is used is essential to addressing scarcity. Agriculture is overwhelmingly the largest consumer, accounting for approximately 70% of all global fresh water withdrawals. Crops such as wheat, rice, cotton, and sugar require enormous quantities of water to grow, and much of the water used in traditional flood or furrow irrigation systems evaporates from the soil surface or runs off before it can be absorbed by plant roots. Industry consumes around 20% of global fresh water, mainly for cooling processes in power generation, and for manufacturing in sectors such as textiles, food processing, and chemicals. Households and municipalities account for only about 10% of total water use, primarily for drinking, cooking, sanitation, and personal hygiene — though this figure rises substantially in high-income countries with water-intensive lifestyles.
Paragraph D
A wide range of strategies is being pursued to ensure that the world has enough fresh water to meet future needs. Modern drip irrigation systems, which deliver water through small emitters placed directly at the base of plants, can reduce agricultural water use by up to 50% compared to conventional methods, without reducing crop yields. Desalination — the process of removing dissolved salts from sea water or brackish water to produce fresh water — is being deployed on a growing scale in water-stressed countries including Saudi Arabia, Israel, and parts of Australia; however, it remains energy-intensive and relatively expensive, limiting its widespread adoption. Water recycling and the treatment of wastewater for reuse in agriculture and industry offer significant potential in many regions. At the policy level, reforming water pricing systems to reflect the true cost of water can encourage more efficient use by farmers, industries, and households alike.

Passage 8: Urban Green Spaces and Environmental Benefits
Topic: Urban Environment & Planning  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
As the world's urban population continues to swell — more than 55% of humanity now lives in cities, a figure projected to reach 68% by 2050 — city planners and environmental scientists are paying increasing attention to the critical role of urban green spaces. Parks, community gardens, street trees, green roofs, wetlands, and urban forests collectively form what ecologists call "urban green infrastructure." Research consistently demonstrates that cities which invest in maintaining and expanding their green spaces tend to enjoy cleaner air, cooler temperatures, better-managed stormwater systems, richer biodiversity, and, importantly, healthier and happier residents. Far from being decorative luxuries, urban green spaces are now understood to be essential components of sustainable, liveable, and resilient cities.
Paragraph B
One of the most important environmental functions of urban vegetation is mitigating the "urban heat island" effect. Cities are typically 2 to 5 degrees Celsius warmer than the surrounding countryside because dark-coloured surfaces — including asphalt roads, concrete pavements, and rooftops — absorb solar radiation and release it as heat. Trees and other plants counteract this warming in two complementary ways. First, through the process of transpiration, in which water is absorbed through roots and released as water vapour through leaf pores, vegetation effectively acts as a natural air-conditioning system. A single mature urban tree can transpire over 400 litres of water per day, with a cooling effect equivalent to running ten air conditioning units for 20 hours. Second, trees and tall plants provide shade that prevents solar radiation from reaching and heating hard surfaces below.
Paragraph C
Urban green spaces also provide crucial stormwater management functions. In heavily urbanised environments, roads, pavements, and buildings cover nearly all ground surfaces, making it nearly impossible for rainwater to infiltrate naturally into the soil. Instead, rainwater runs off quickly into drainage systems, which can become overwhelmed during heavy rainfall events, leading to urban flooding. Parks, gardens, and especially urban wetlands act as natural sponges, absorbing and slowly releasing large volumes of rainwater, significantly reducing peak runoff flows. Street trees and vegetation strips along roads slow the movement of rainwater and allow more to soak into the ground. Additionally, as water filters through vegetated soil, many pollutants and contaminants are naturally removed before the water reaches rivers, aquifers, or the sea, improving overall water quality.
Paragraph D
Beyond their environmental benefits, urban green spaces are increasingly recognised for their profound effects on human physical and mental health and on social cohesion. Numerous studies have found that regular access to parks and nature significantly reduces levels of stress hormones such as cortisol, lowers blood pressure, and decreases rates of depression and anxiety. Green spaces encourage physical activity — walking, cycling, sport, and play — that reduces the risk of obesity, heart disease, and diabetes. However, access to urban green spaces is far from equal: in most cities, wealthy neighbourhoods enjoy substantially more parkland, tree cover, and garden space than poorer communities. Urban planners and social equity advocates argue that equitable distribution of green infrastructure is not merely an environmental policy issue, but a matter of social justice and public health that affects the quality of life of the most disadvantaged urban residents.

Passage 9: The Impact of Fast Fashion on the Environment
Topic: Consumer Culture & Sustainability  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Few industries have transformed as dramatically — or as destructively — in the past three decades as the global fashion industry. The rise of "fast fashion" — a business model based on rapidly producing large quantities of cheap, trend-driven clothing designed to be used briefly and discarded — has fundamentally altered the economics and environmental footprint of clothing production. Leading fast-fashion brands now release new collections every few weeks rather than the traditional two seasons per year, encouraging consumers to buy more and discard more than ever before. Global clothing production doubled between 2000 and 2015, and the average consumer now purchases 60% more garments than 15 years ago, while keeping each item for only half as long. The result is staggering levels of textile waste: it is estimated that the equivalent of one rubbish truck of clothing is discarded or incinerated every second somewhere in the world.
Paragraph B
The water footprint of the fashion industry is enormous. Cotton — the world's most widely used natural fibre — is an extremely thirsty crop, requiring an estimated 10,000 litres of water to produce just one kilogram of raw cotton fibre. This translates to approximately 2,700 litres of water consumed in the production of a single cotton T-shirt — roughly equivalent to the amount an average person drinks over two and a half years. The production of cotton also frequently involves the heavy use of pesticides, which can contaminate local water supplies and harm both ecosystems and farmworkers. Synthetic fabrics such as polyester and nylon, which now make up about 60% of all clothing produced globally, are derived from petroleum and present a different but equally serious problem: each time a synthetic garment is washed, it releases thousands of tiny plastic fibres — known as microfibres — into wastewater systems, which eventually reach rivers, lakes, and oceans, contributing significantly to microplastic pollution.
Paragraph C
The fashion industry is also a major contributor to greenhouse gas emissions and climate change. The sector is estimated to produce around 10% of all global annual carbon dioxide emissions — more than the combined emissions from all international aviation and maritime shipping. Most clothing is manufactured in countries such as Bangladesh, Vietnam, Cambodia, and China, where factories frequently rely on coal-generated electricity and where labour and environmental regulations may be weaker. The dyeing and finishing of textiles is another highly polluting process: it is estimated that textile dyeing is the second-largest polluter of clean water globally, after agriculture, releasing toxic chemicals including heavy metals, formaldehyde, and chlorinated compounds into waterways near factories.
Paragraph D
The search for more sustainable alternatives to fast fashion is gathering momentum from both consumers and businesses. The "slow fashion" movement advocates for buying fewer, higher-quality, longer-lasting garments, prioritising local production and ethically sourced materials. The secondhand clothing market — which includes charity shops, vintage stores, and online resale platforms such as Vinted and Depop — has grown explosively and is projected to exceed the fast fashion market in value by 2030. Some forward-thinking brands are investing in textile recycling technologies that break down old garments to create new fabric fibres, reducing the need for virgin raw materials. Clothing rental services, which allow consumers to borrow and return items rather than purchasing them outright, are also gaining popularity, particularly for occasional-wear items. However, many experts argue that without binding government regulation — including mandatory product labelling, extended producer responsibility schemes, and restrictions on the most environmentally harmful materials — voluntary industry commitments and individual consumer choices will be insufficient to transform the fashion system at the scale and speed required.

Passage 10: Sustainable Agriculture — Feeding the Future
Topic: Food Systems & Environment  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The global food system faces an enormous dual challenge in the coming decades: feeding a world population that is projected to reach nearly 10 billion by 2050, while simultaneously reducing the enormous environmental damage that modern agriculture currently causes. Conventional intensive farming — which relies heavily on synthetic fertilisers derived from fossil fuels, broad-spectrum pesticides, large-scale irrigation systems, and the genetic modification of crops for high yields — has been remarkably successful at increasing food production over the past 70 years. However, this success has come at a significant cost. Industrial agriculture is responsible for approximately 26% of all global greenhouse gas emissions, is the primary driver of deforestation globally, uses about 70% of all fresh water withdrawn by humans, and has caused the loss of around one third of the world's topsoil in the past century through erosion and degradation. Finding ways to produce enough food without destroying the natural systems upon which food production itself depends is one of the most pressing sustainability challenges of our time.
Paragraph B
Sustainable agriculture encompasses a diverse range of farming practices and systems that aim to produce food in environmentally responsible and economically viable ways while supporting the long-term health of agricultural soils and ecosystems. Organic farming represents one widely adopted approach: it eliminates synthetic fertilisers and pesticides entirely, relying instead on organic matter — compost, green manures, and animal manures — to enrich the soil, and on biological methods such as beneficial insects and crop diversity to manage pests. Agroforestry integrates trees and shrubs into crop and livestock systems, improving soil structure, increasing carbon storage, and providing additional income streams for farmers. Crop rotation — the practice of growing different types of crops in sequence on the same land — breaks pest and disease cycles, reduces the need for chemical inputs, and maintains soil fertility by varying nutrient demands and replenishment.
Paragraph C
Emerging technologies offer significant potential to transform the efficiency and sustainability of food production. Precision agriculture applies advanced data analytics, GPS mapping, remote sensing via satellites and drones, and sensor networks embedded in fields to monitor soil conditions, crop health, and water and nutrient requirements in real time. By applying fertilisers, pesticides, and water only where and when they are genuinely needed, precision agriculture can dramatically reduce chemical and water inputs compared to blanket application across entire fields. Vertical farming — the growing of crops in stacked, climate-controlled indoor environments using LED lighting optimised for plant growth — can produce yields up to 100 times higher per square metre than outdoor farming, using up to 95% less water and no pesticides. While the high energy costs of indoor farming remain a challenge, falling costs of LED technology and renewable energy are making vertical farms increasingly viable, particularly for high-value salad crops, herbs, and strawberries.
Paragraph D
Transforming what people eat is widely considered just as important as changing how food is produced. The environmental costs of animal-based foods, particularly beef and dairy, are substantially higher than those of plant-based alternatives: producing one kilogram of beef generates roughly 60 kilograms of greenhouse gas emissions, while producing one kilogram of tofu generates fewer than 4 kilograms. Global dietary shifts towards plant-rich diets featuring more vegetables, legumes, nuts, and whole grains — while reducing meat and dairy consumption, particularly in high-income countries — could reduce food-system emissions by up to 50%. Alternative protein sources are also gaining scientific and commercial traction: insect farming produces high-quality protein with a fraction of the land, water, and greenhouse gas footprint of conventional livestock; and cultivated meat — genuine animal muscle tissue grown from cells in bioreactors, without slaughtering animals — is approaching commercial viability in several countries. However, experts consistently caution that food system transformation requires not just technological innovation and changes in consumer behaviour, but also supportive government policies, international cooperation, and changes in the economic incentives that currently make unsustainable food choices cheaper than sustainable ones.
